title: Voisin ottaa vanilla latte mukaan
subtitle: I'd like a vanilla latte to go

blocks.text: You heard this at a café counter. It sounds long and intimidating — but it's actually three clean pieces snapped together. By the end of this lesson you'll be ordering anything in any Finnish café, politely, like a local.

blocks.annotated: {Voisin|grammar|conditional|I could/I'd like} {ottaa|word|verb|to take/have} vanilla latte {mukaan|word|adverb|to go}.
blocks.translation: I'd like a vanilla latte to go.

blocks.title: Why not just say haluan — I want?
blocks.text: Finns use the conditional voisin (I could) instead of haluan (I want) when ordering. It's softer and more polite — the Finnish equivalent of saying 'I'd like' instead of 'I want.' Using haluan at a counter isn't rude, but voisin is what you'll hear from Finns themselves.

blocks.content: {Voisin|grammar|conditional|I could} comes from {voida|word|verb|to be able to / can}. Add the ending -isin and it becomes conditional: 'I could.' In ordering contexts it just means 'I'd like.' You don't need to understand the grammar fully yet — just own the word.

blocks.title: Pattern: Voisin + ottaa + [thing]
blocks.text: Voisin ottaa literally means 'I could take.' In Finnish café culture, ottaa (to take) is the natural verb for ordering food and drink — you take a coffee, you take a sandwich. Not 'have,' not 'get' — take.
Piece 2 — Ottaa: to take / to order

blocks.title: Wait — kahvi became kahvin?
blocks.text: Yes. When you order or use a noun as a direct object in Finnish, it picks up an -n ending. kahvi → kahvin, tee → teen, vesi → veden. It looks scary but you'll absorb it naturally. For now, just notice the pattern and don't stress it.

blocks.content: {Mukaan|word|adverb|to go / along with} literally means 'along' or 'with me.' In a café, it means to go / takeaway. Drop it and you're eating in. Add it and you're walking out with your cup.

items.en: I'd like a coffee to go.
I'd like a vanilla latte to go.
I'd like a tea. (eating in, no 'to go')

lines.speaker: A
lines.en: Hi! What can I get you?

lines.speaker: B
lines.fi: Hei! Voisin ottaa vanilla latte mukaan.
lines.en: Hi! I'd like a vanilla latte to go.

blocks.title: Free words from the dialogue
blocks.text: Hei = Hi. Kiitos = Thank you (memorise this immediately). Iso = large/big. Mikä koko? = What size? Nimesi = your name (formal version of sun nimi — you'll hear both).
Now swap the drink — order anything

items.en: I'd like a cappuccino to go.
I'd like a cinnamon roll to go.

blocks.title: Korvapuusti — the Finnish cinnamon roll
blocks.text: Literally 'ear slap.' It's the iconic Finnish cardamom-and-cinnamon bun, bigger and denser than the Swedish kanelbulle. If you're in a Finnish café, you're ordering one. Voisin ottaa korvapuustin.

blocks.content: Voisin: VOY-sin (the oi is like 'boy'). Ottaa: OT-taa (double t = a brief stop, both a's are long and pure). Mukaan: MOO-kaan (long u, long aa — Finnish vowels never swallow themselves).

blocks.title: What you now own
items: voisin ottaa — I'd like (the polite ordering formula)
You can now order any drink or food in a Finnish café
You understand why Finns say voisin instead of haluan
